summary: Stancil argues that BI, like early software, has been unbundled and stripped of legacy connotations, leaving today’s BI as a diminished fragment focused on visualization and reporting. He contends the next generation of BI should be a universal consumption layer that integrates self-serve and deep analysis, sits atop shared governance, and centers collaboration between analysts and non-analysts.
money_quotes: It’s as though we took our definition of BI from twenty years ago and started crossing off clauses, until we’re left with “visualization and reporting.”
BI tools should aspire to do one thing, and do it completely: They should be the universal tool for people to consume and make sense of data.
Eventually, a semantic layer will be a bug, not a feature.
By defining BI as just self-serve, we shortcut what it could be, no matter how good that self-serve is.
The old version of BI—a complete end-to-end stack, responsible for nearly all of a company’s analytical needs—is already dead.
key_insight: BI should evolve from a fragmented, report-centric tool into a universal data-consumption platform that unifies self-serve and deep analysis while relying on shared governance layers.
